preFix.title: Polymer clay gradient blend earrings
preFix.subtitle: Teardrop earrings with a smooth two-colour gradient blend
preFix.excerpt: A pair of teardrop earrings made from polymer clay using a Skinner-style gradient blend between two colours. The clay is folded and rolled repeatedly to create a smooth transition from one colour to the other across the finished shape. No kiln required; bakes in a domestic oven. Suitable for pierced ears.
preFix.sourceNotes: Gradient blend technique derived from first principles. The folding-blend method is a fundamental polymer clay technique.

content.text: A gradient blend is one of the most satisfying colour effects in
polymer clay
. Two contrasting colours are folded together in a
Skinner blend

until the transition from one colour to the other is smooth and continuous. These teardrop earrings show off the full gradient from top to bottom. The method works with any two colours; the strongest gradients come from colours that mix to make a third colour (blue and yellow through green; red and white through pink).
Conditioning
Condition each colour separately by kneading until pliable with no cracks at the edges. Condition both colours to the same firmness so they blend evenly.
Building the gradient

Roll each colour into a flat square sheet about 3 mm thick. Cut each square diagonally to make two triangles. Take one triangle of colour A and one triangle of colour B and place them together with the long diagonal edges touching to make a rectangle: colour A on the left, colour B on the right. Press the diagonal join firmly.

Fold the rectangle in half bringing the left edge to the right edge. Press the fold flat. Roll out to the original rectangle dimensions. Fold again, same direction. Repeat this fold-and-roll process 20-25 times. After the first 10 folds the blend will appear blotchy and uneven. Continue: the blend smooths out around fold 15-20 and becomes a clean gradient by fold 25.
Cutting the earring shapes

Roll the finished gradient slab to 3 mm thickness. The gradient should now run smoothly from one colour at one end to the other colour at the other end. Cut two matching teardrop shapes using a template or a teardrop cutter, each about 25 mm long by 15 mm wide. Align the cutter so the gradient runs from top to bottom of each teardrop.
Adding the holes and curing
Pierce a hole near the narrow top of each teardrop with the needle tool. Bake on a dedicated oven liner at 110-130°C for 20 minutes. Allow to cool fully before handling.
Finishing
Sand with 400-grit and then 800-grit sandpaper for a smooth polished surface. Buff with a soft cloth. Thread a jump ring through each hole and attach an earring hook finding.
Care
Polymer clay jewellery is decorative. Not food-safe. Avoid prolonged direct sunlight, which can cause some pigments to fade over time. Store in a jewellery box away from heat.
Variations
Cut the gradient slab into circular studs for a simpler earring shape.
Use three colours by blending A into B and B into C in two separate steps, then joining the two gradient slabs end-to-end.
Make a matching pendant by cutting a larger teardrop from the same slab.

rows.fix: Continue folding. The blend always looks blotchy in the middle of the process and smooths out at the end.
rows.cause: The two clay colours are too similar in firmness and blended unevenly, or fewer than 20 folds were completed

rows.fix: Bake on a flat ceramic tile. Place a flat piece of card on top of the teardrops during the first 10 minutes of curing to keep them flat.
rows.cause: Resting on a curved surface in the oven, or clay was not pressed flat before cutting

rows.fix: Insert a toothpick through the hole before placing in the oven. Remove the toothpick after curing.
rows.cause: The clay softened around the hole before curing was complete

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
